results.possibleIdentity: Gordon Fitch is a New York-based conceptual artist, writer, and publisher who has been active in the city's art scene since the late 1960s or early 1970s. He is the publisher of Artezine (A Cyberspace Review of the Arts), which he established in 1999. Fitch was a contemporary of the SoHo "Downtown Scene" and has been associated with figures such as Virginia Admiral and the artist settlement at 226 Lafayette Street. His artistic practice involves conceptual and performance-based works, including "self-destroying" art pieces such as the "Great Cookie" project at Flux Factory in 2014, inspired by the 50th anniversary of Jean Tinguely's "Homage to New York." Fitch is also noted for his involvement with Minerva Durham's Spring Studio, a prominent figure drawing studio in New York. His background aligns with the Experimental in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) community due to his long-standing interest in the intersection of art, conceptualism, and technology, as well as his early presence in digital/networking circles (appearing in historical host lists such as Telehack). Additionally, a Gordon Fitch is credited as a songwriter in the mid-1960s, notably for the anti-war song "August Moon" featured in Sing Out! Magazine in 1966.

results.possibleIdentity: Lucio Pozzi (born 1935) is an Italian-American multidisciplinary artist known for his extensive work in painting, sculpture, video, performance, and installation. Born in Milan, he moved to the United States in 1962 as a guest of the Harvard International Summer Seminar and subsequently settled in New York City. Pozzi was active in the New York avant-garde scene during the late 1960s and 1970s, where he was associated with movements such as Minimalism, Conceptual Art, Fluxus, and Arte Povera. He was involved with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), an organization that fostered collaborations between artists and engineers; his name appears in the E.A.T. records regarding artist/engineer matches made between 1966 and 1968. His career includes being a co-founder of the influential art journal October (1976) and the journal New Observations. His early work includes series such as 'Triangles-Red' (1969) and the 'Level Group' (early 1970s). In 1978, the Museum of Modern Art featured his early videotapes in one of the first solo exhibitions of its Projects: Video series. He has exhibited at major international venues, including Documenta 6 (1977) and the Venice Biennale (1980). Pozzi has also had a significant teaching career at institutions such as Cooper Union, Yale University, Princeton University, and the School of Visual Arts.

results.possibleIdentity: Jean Toche (1932–2018), born Jean Xavier Van Imschoot in Bruges, Belgium, was a Belgian-American conceptual artist, poet, and radical activist primarily active in New York City from the mid-1960s through the 1970s. Originally an abstract expressionist in the 1950s, he immigrated to the U.S. in 1965 and became a central figure in the "Destruction Art" movement and the radical political art scene. In October 1969, he co-founded the Guerrilla Art Action Group (GAAG) with Jon Hendricks and Poppy Johnson, which performed provocative, non-violent "actions" to protest the Vietnam War and the perceived corruption of art institutions like the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA). One of their most famous actions, "A Blood Bath" (1969), involved spilling animal blood in the MoMA lobby to protest the museum's ties to the Rockefeller family and the defense industry. Toche was also a member of the Art Workers Coalition (AWC) and co-organized "The People's Flag Show" in 1970, for which he was arrested on charges of flag desecration. In 1974, he was arrested by the FBI after mailing a flyer to the Metropolitan Museum of Art that was interpreted as a kidnapping threat against its trustees. Toche's work is documented in the archive of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), specifically noted in records from 1968. In his later years, he focused on mail art and politically charged photo-text combinations.

results.possibleIdentity: Walter Wright is a pioneering video artist and animator who was active in the late 1960s and early 1970s. His work is characterized by the intersection of art and technology, specifically in the realms of computer graphics, video synthesis, and live performance. Wright began his career in the mid-1960s, working with early 3D software and CAD programs at the University of Waterloo around 1966. He was deeply influenced by the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) group and the research being conducted at Bell Laboratories. In 1969, he completed "Vidium," a color X/Y display controlled by sound input. By 1972, he became an associate director at The Kitchen in New York and served as one of the first artists-in-residence at the Experimental Television Center (ETC) from 1973 to 1976. He is known for his work with the Paik-Abe Video Synthesizer and the Scanimate analog computer, and he pioneered the genre of live video performance, often collaborating with electronic music composers and artists such as Nam June Paik, Ed Emshwiller, and Sun Ra.

results.possibleIdentity: William Soghor (often appearing as W. Soghor) is an artist and service provider to the art community who was active in New York City during the late 1960s and early 1970s. He is known for his participation in the significant group exhibition 'Soft as Art' held at the New York Cultural Center in 1973, where he exhibited alongside prominent figures such as Robert Rauschenberg, Claes Oldenburg, Richard Serra, and Robert Mapplethorpe. His association with Rauschenberg, a founder of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), and his presence in the avant-garde New York art scene of that era align him with the milieu of E.A.T. Beyond his work as a fine artist, he founded 'The William Soghor Company,' which provided technical and archival solutions for artists, most notably specialized artwork inventory file cards designed to hold 35mm slides—a service that reflects the E.A.T. organization's goal of fostering practical and technical support for the creative community. Soghor was based in Manhattan and was part of a family deeply embedded in the arts; he was the brother-in-law of art historian Doris Soghor and is married to the realist painter Phyllis Herfield.

results.possibleIdentity: Thomas Titolo (often referred to as T. Titolo) was an engineer at Bell Laboratories and a key technical collaborator and photographer associated with the non-profit organization Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) during the late 1960s and 1970s. He provided engineering expertise and was notably involved in the realization of the Pepsi Pavilion at Expo '70 in Osaka, Japan. Beyond his technical contributions, Titolo is frequently credited as a photographer who documented major E.A.T. projects and exhibitions, including the 'Some More Beginnings' show at the Brooklyn Museum in 1968. His work served to bridge the gap between scientific engineering and artistic practice, a core mission of E.A.T.

results.possibleIdentity: Jean Dupuy (1925–2021) was a French-born artist who became a prominent figure in the New York avant-garde scene after moving there in 1967. He is best known for his work at the intersection of art and technology, specifically his association with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization. In 1968, he won an E.A.T. competition for his 'dust sculpture' titled 'Cone Pyramid' (also known as 'Heart Beats Dust'), which used a stethoscope to vibrate red lithol rubine dust in response to a viewer's heartbeat. This work was famously exhibited at the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA) and the Brooklyn Museum. During the late 1960s and 1970s, Dupuy was a prolific organizer of collective performances and happenings, curating events at his studio on 13th Street, The Kitchen, and Judson Church. He was closely associated with the Fluxus movement and artists such as George Maciunas and Nam June Paik. His later work often involved anagrams and wordplay.
